author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

ccel.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

title: Homily XXI on Acts ix. 26, 27.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Saul was come to Jerusalem, he assayed to join himself to the disciples: but they were all afraid of him, and believed not that he was a disciple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Barnabas took him, and brought him to the Apostles, and declared unto them how he had seen the Lord in the way.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: One may well be much at a loss here to understand how it is that, whereas in the Epistle to the Galatians Paul says, “I went not to Jerusalem,” but “into Arabia” and “to Damascus,” and, “After three years I went up to Jerusalem,” and “to see Peter” (Gal. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), (ἱστορἥσαι Cat.) here the writer says the contrary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (There, Paul says,) “And none of the Apostles saw I; but here, it is said (Barnabas), brought him to the Apostles.”—Well, then, either (Paul) means, “I went not up with intent to refer or attach myself to them (ἀναθέσθαι)—for what saith he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I referred not myself, neither went I to Jerusalem to those who were Apostles before me:” or else, that the laying await for him in Damascus was after his return from Arabia; or else, again, that the visit to Jerusalem was after he came from Arabia.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Certainly of his own accord he went not to the Apostles, but “assayed to join himself unto the disciples”—as being a teacher, not a disciple—“I went not,” he says, “for this purpose, that I should go to those who were Apostles before me: certainly, I learnt nothing from them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or, he does not speak of this visit, but passes it by, so that the order is, “I went into Arabia, then I came to Damascus, then to Jerusalem, then to Syria:” or else, again, that he went up to Jerusalem, then was sent to Damascus, then to Arabia, then again to Damascus, then to Cæsarea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also, the visit “after fourteen years,” probably, was when he brought up the [alms to the] brethren together with Barnabas: (Gal. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1) or else he means a different occasion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Historian for conciseness, often omits incidents, and condenses the times.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how unambitious the writer is, and how he does not even relate (related in ch. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17–21) that vision, but passes it by.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He assayed,” it says, “to join himself to the disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they were afraid of him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By this again is shown the ardor of Paul’s character: not (only) from the mouth of Ananias, and of those who wondered at him there, but also of those in Jerusalem: “they believed not that he was a disciple:” for truly that was beyond all human expectation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was no longer a wild beast, but a man mild and gentle!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how he does not go to the Apostles, such is his forbearance, but to the disciples, as being a disciple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was not thought worthy of credit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Barnabas”—“Son of Consolation” is his appellation, whence also he makes himself easy of access to the man: for “he was a kind man” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24), exceedingly, and this is proved both by the present instance, and in the affair of John (Mark)—“having taken him, brought him to the Apostles, and related to them how he had seen the Lord in the way.” (xv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is likely that at Damascus also he had heard all about him: whence he was not afraid but the others were, for he was a man whose glance inspired fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “How,” it says, “he had seen the Lord in the way, and that He had spoken unto him, and how in Damascus he had spoken boldly in the name of the Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he was with them coming in and going out at Jerusalem, and speaking boldly in the name of Jesus” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 28): these things were demonstrative of the former, and by his acts he made good what was spoken of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he spake, and disputed with the Hellenists.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then the disciples were afraid of him, and the Apostles did not trust him; by this therefore he relieves them of their fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With the Hellenists:” he means those who used the Greek tongue: and this he did, very wisely; for those others, those profound Hebrews had no mind even to see him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But they,” it says, “went about to slay him:” a token, this, of his energy, and triumphant victory, and of their exceeding annoyance at what had happened.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thereupon, fearing lest the issue should be the same as in the case of Stephen, they sent him to Cæsarea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it says, “When the brethren were aware of this, they brought him down to Cæsarea, and sent him forth to Tarsus” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 30), at the same time to preach, and likely to be more in safety, as being in his own country.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe, I pray you, how far it is from being the case that everything is done by (miraculous) grace; how, on the contrary, God does in many things leave them to manage for themselves by their own wisdom and in a human way; so to cut off the excuse of idle people: for if it was so in the case of Paul, much more in theirs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then, it says, “the Church throughout all Judea and Galilee and Samaria had peace (they), being edified, and walking in the fear of the Lord, and abounded in the comfort of the Holy Ghost.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is about to relate that Peter goes down (from Jerusalem), therefore that you may not impute this to fear, he first says this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For while there was persecution, he was in Jerusalem, but when the affairs of the Church are everywhere in security, then it is that he leaves Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how fervent and energetic he is!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he did not think, because there was peace, therefore there was no need of his presence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul departed, and there was peace: there is no war nor disturbance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Them, they respected most, as having often stood by them, and as being held in admiration by the multitude: but him, they despised, and were more savage against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, how great a war, and immediately, peace!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what that war effected.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It dispersed the peace-makers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In Samaria, Simon was put to shame: in Judea, the affair of Sapphira took place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not that, because there was peace, therefore matters became relaxed, but such was the peace as also to need exhortation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, as Peter passed throughout all quarters, he came down also to the saints which dwelt at Lydda.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like the commander of an army, he went about, inspecting the ranks, what part was compact, what in good order, what needed his presence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how on all occasions he goes about, foremost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When an Apostle was to be chosen, he was the foremost: when the Jews were to be told, that these were “not drunken,” when the lame man was to be healed, when harangues to be made, he is before the rest: when the rulers were to be spoken to, he was the man; when Ananias, he (ch. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 3–15.): when healings were wrought by the shadow, still it was he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And look: where there was danger, he was the man, and where good management (was needed); but where all is calm, there they act all in common, and he demands no greater honor (than the others).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When need was to work miracles, he starts forward, and here again he is the man to labor and toil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there he found a certain man named Æneas, which had kept his bed eight years, and was sick of the palsy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Peter said unto him, Æneas, Jesus Christ maketh thee whole: arise, and make thy bed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he arose immediately.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why did he not wait for the man’s faith, and ask if he wished to be healed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the first place, the miracle served for exhortation to many: hear then how great the gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all that dwelt at Lydda and Saron saw him, and turned to the Lord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the man was notable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Arise, and make thy bed:” he does well to give a proof of the miracle: for they not only released men of their diseases, but in giving the health they gave the strength also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, at that time they had given no proofs of their power, so that the man could not reasonably have been required to show his faith, as neither in the case of the lame man did they demand it. (ch. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As therefore Christ in the beginning of His miracles did not demand faith, so neither did these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in Jerusalem indeed, as was but reasonable, the faith of the parties was first shown; “they brought out their sick into the streets, but as Peter passed by, his shadow at least might fall upon some of them” (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15); for many miracles had been wrought there; but here this is the first that occurs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of the miracles, some were wrought for the purpose of drawing others (to faith); some for the comfort of them that believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now there was at Joppa a certain disciple named Tabitha, which by interpretation is called Dorcas: this woman was full of good works and alms-deeds which she did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it came to pass in those days, that she was sick, and died: whom when they had washed, they laid her in an upper chamber.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And forasmuch as Lydda was nigh to Joppa, and the disciples had heard that Peter was there, they sent unto him two men, desiring him that he would not delay to come to them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did they wait till she was dead?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why was not Peter solicited (ἐσκύλη) before this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So right-minded (φιλοσοφοὕντες) were they, they did not think it proper to trouble (σκύλλειν) the Disciples about such matters, and to take them away from the preaching: as indeed this is why it mentions that the place was near, seeing they asked this as a thing beside his mark, and not now in the regular course.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Not to delay to come unto them:” for she was a disciple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Peter arose, and went with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he was come, they led him into the upper chamber.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They do not beseech, but leave it to him to give her life (σωτηρίαν.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what a cheering inducement to alms is here!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all the widows,” it says, “stood round him weeping, and showing the coats and garments which Dorcas had made while she was with them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Peter went into the apartment, as one who took it calmly, but see what an accession came of it!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not without a meaning that the Writer has informed us of the woman’s name, but to show that the name she bore (φερώνυμος ἦν) matched her character; as active and wakeful was she as an antelope.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in many instances there is a Providence in the giving of names, as we have often told you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “She was full,” it says, “of good works:” not only of alms, but “of good works,” first, and then of this good work in particular.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which,” it says, “Dorcas made while she was with them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great humility!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not as we do; but they were all together in common, and in company with them she made these things and worked.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Peter put them all forth, and kneeled down, and prayed; and turning him to the body said, Tabitha, arise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And she opened her eyes: and when she saw Peter, she sat up.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does he put them all out?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That he may not be confused nor disturbed by their weeping.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having knelt down, he prayed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the intentness of his prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he gave her his hand.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So did Christ to the daughter of Jairus: “And (says the Evangelist) having taken her by the hand.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark severally, first the life, then the strength brought into her, the one by the word, the other by his hand—“And he gave her his hand, and lifted her up, and when he had called the saints and widows, presented her alive:” to some for comfort, because they received back their sister, and because they saw the miracle, and for kindly support (προστασίαν) to others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it was known throughout all Joppa; and many believed in the Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it came to pass, that he tarried many days in Joppa with one Simon a tanner.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the unassuming conduct, mark the moderation of Peter, how he does not make his abode with this lady, or some other person of distinction, but with a tanner: by all his acts leading men to humility, neither suffering the mean to be ashamed, nor the great to be elated!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Many days;” for they needed his instruction, who had believed through the miracles.—Let us look then again at what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Assayed,” it says, “to join himself to the disciples.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not come up to them unabashed, but with a subdued manner.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Disciples” they were all called at that time by reason of their great virtue, for there was the likeness of the disciples plainly to be seen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But they were all afraid of him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how they feared the dangers, how the alarm was yet at its height in them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Barnabas,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27.)—it seems to me that Barnabas was of old a friend of his—“and related,” etc.: observe how Paul says nothing of all this himself: nor would he have brought it forward to the others, had he not been compelled to do so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he was with them, coming in and going out at Jerusalem, and speaking boldly in the name of the Lord Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This gave them all confidence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But they went about to slay him: which when the brethren knew” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe how both there (at Damascus), and here, the rest take care for him, and provide for him the means of departure, and that we nowhere find him thus far receiving (direct supernatural) aid from God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So the energy of his character is betokened.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To Cæsarea, and sent him forth to Tarsus:” so that, I suppose, he did not continue his journey by land, but sailed the rest of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this (departure) is Providentially ordered, that he might preach there also: and so likewise were the plots against him ordered by God’s Providence, and his coming to Jerusalem, that the story about him might no longer be disbelieved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there he was “speaking boldly,” it says, “in the name of the Lord Jesus; and he spake and disputed against the Hellenists;” and again, “he was with them coming in and going out.—So the Church throughout all Judea and Galilee and Samaria had peace”—i.e. it increased: and peace with itself, that peace which is peace indeed: for the war from without would have done them no harm —“they being edified, and walking in the fear of the Lord, and abounded in the consolation of the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the spirit consoled them both by the miracles and by the works, and independently of these in the person of each individual.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, etc. And Peter said unto him, Eneas,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But before discourse, before exhortations, he says to the lame man himself, “Jesus Christ maketh thee whole.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This word he believed in any wise, and was made whole.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how unassuming he is: for he said not, “In the Name,” but rather as a sign he narrates the miracle itself, and speaks as its Evangelist.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having seen him,” it says, “all that dwelt in Lydda, and Saron, turned unto the Lord.—Now there was at Joppa,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe everywhere the signs taking place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us so believe them, as if we were now beholding them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not simply said, that Tabitha died, but that she died, having been in a state of weakness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And (yet) they did not call Peter until she died; then “they sent and told him not to delay to come unto them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, they send and call him by others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he comes: he did not think it a piece of disrespect, to be summoned by two men: for, it says, “they sent two men unto him.”—Affliction, my beloved, is a great thing, and rivets our souls together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not a word of wailing there, nor of mourning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how thoroughly matters are cleansed!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having washed her,” it says, “they laid her in an upper chamber:” that is, they did all (that was right) for the dead body.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Peter having come, “knelt down, and prayed; and turning him to the body, said, Tabitha, arise.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not perform all their miracles with the same ease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this was profitable for them: for truly God took thought not only for the salvation of others, but for their own.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that healed so many by his very shadow, how is it that he now has to do so much first?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There are cases also in which the faith of the applicants coöperated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the first dead person that he raises.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he, as it were, awakes her out of sleep: first she opened her eyes: then upon seeing (Peter) she sat up: then from his hand she received strength.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it was known throughout all Joppa, and many believed in the Lord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the gain, mark the fruit, that it was not for display.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Indeed, this is why he puts them all out, imitating his Master in this also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For where tears are—or rather, where miracles are, there tears ought not to be; not where such a mystery is celebrating.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear, I beseech you: although somewhat of the like kind does not take place now, yet in the case of our dead likewise, a great mystery is celebrating.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, if as we sit together, the Emperor were to send and invite some one of us to the palace, would it be right, I ask, to weep and mourn?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Angels are present, commissioned from heaven and come from thence, sent from the King Himself to call their fellow servant, and say, dost thou weep?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowest thou not what a mystery it is that is taking place, how awful, how dread, and worthy indeed of hymns and lauds?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wouldest thou learn, that thou mayest know, that this is no time for tears?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is a very great mystery of the Wisdom of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As if leaving her dwelling, the soul goes forth, speeding on her way to her own Lord, and dost thou mourn?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, thou shouldst do this on the birth of a child: for this in fact is also a birth, and a better than that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For here she goes forth to a very different light, is loosed as from a prison-house, comes off as from a contest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yes,” say you, “it is all very well to say this, in the case of those of whose salvation we are assured.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, what ails thee, O man, that even in the case of such, thou dost not take it in this way?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, what canst thou have to condemn in the little child?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou mourn for it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What in the newly baptized? for he too is brought into the same condition: why dost thou mourn for him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as the sun arises clear and bright, so the soul, leaving the body with a pure conscience, shines joyously.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not such the spectacle of Emperor as he comes in state to take possession of the city (ἐπιβαίνοντα πόλεως), not such the hush of awe, as when the soul having quitted the body is departing in company with Angels.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think what the soul must then be! in what amazement, what wonder, what delight!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why mournest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me.—But it is only in the case of sinners thou doest this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would that it were so, and I would not forbid your mournings, would that this were the object!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This lamentation were Apostolic, this were after the pattern of the Lord; for even Jesus wept over Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would that your mournings were discriminated by this rule.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when thou speakest the words of one that would call back (the dead), and speakest of thy long intimacy and his beneficence, it is but for this thou mournest (not because he was a sinner), thou dost but pretend to say it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mourn, bewail the sinner, and I too will give a loose to tears; I, more than thou, the greater the punishment to which he is liable as such: I too will lament, with such an object.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not thou alone must lament him that is such; the whole city must do the same, and all that meet you on the way, as men bewail them that are led to be put to death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is a death indeed, an evil death, the death of sinners.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But (with you) all is clean reversed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such lamentation marks a lofty mind, and conveys much instruction; the other marks a littleness of soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we all lamented with this sort of lamentation, we should amend the persons themselves while yet living.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as, if it rested with thee to apply medicines which would prevent that bodily death, thou wouldest use them, just so now, if this death were the death thou lamentest, thou wouldest prevent its taking place, both in thyself and in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereas now our behavior is a perfect riddle; that having it in our power to hinder its coming, we let it take place, and mourn over it when it has come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Worthy indeed of lamentations are they (when we consider), what time as they shall stand before the judgment seat of Christ, what words they shall then hear, what they shall suffer!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To no purpose have these men lived: nay, not to no purpose, but to evil purpose!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of them too it may be fitly said, “It were good for them had they never been born.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what profit is it, I ask, to have spent so much time to the hurt of his own person?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had it been spent only to no purpose, were not that, I ask you, punishment enough!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If one who has been an hired servant twenty years were to find that he has had all his labor in vain, would he not weep and lament, and think himself the most miserable of men?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, here is a man who has lost all the labor of a whole life: not one day has he lived for himself, but to luxury, to debauchery, to covetousness, to sin, to the devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, say, shall we not bewail this man? shall we not try to snatch him from his perils?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is, yes, it is possible, if we will, to mitigate his punishment, if we make continual prayers for him, if for him we give alms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However unworthy he may be, God will yield to our importunity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if Paul showed mercy on one (who had no claims on his mercy), and for the sake of others spared one (whom he would not have spared), much more is it right for us to do this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By means of his substance, by means of thine own, by what means thou wilt, aid him: pour in oil, nay rather, water.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has he no alms-deeds of his own to exhibit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let him have at least those of his kindred.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has he none done by himself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At least let him have those which are done for him, that his wife may with confidence beg him off in that day, having paid down the ransom for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The more sins he has to answer for, the greater need has he of alms, not only for this reason, but because the alms has not the same virtue now, but far less: for it is not all one to have done it himself, and to have another do it for him; therefore, the virtue being less, let us by quantity make it the greatest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not busy ourselves about monuments, not about memorials.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the greatest memorial: set widows to stand around him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell them his name: bid them all make for him their prayers, their supplications: this will overcome God: though it have not been done by the man himself, yet because of him another is the author of the almsgiving.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even this pertains to the mercy of God: “widows standing around and weeping” know how to rescue, not indeed from the present death, but from that which is to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many have profited even by the alms done by others on their behalf: for even if they have not got perfect (deliverance), at least they have found some comfort thence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If it be not so, how are children saved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet there, the children themselves contribute nothing, but their parents do all: and often have women had their children given them, though the children themselves contributed nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many are the ways God gives us to be saved, only let us not be negligent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then if one be poor? say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again I say, the greatness of the alms is not estimated by the quantity given, but by the purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Only give not less than thine ability, and thou hast paid all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then, say you, if he be desolate and a stranger, and have none to care for him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why is it that he has none, I ask you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In this very thing thou sufferest thy desert, that thou hast none to be thus thy friend, thus virtuous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is so ordered on purpose that, though we be not ourselves virtuous, we may study to have virtuous companions and friends—both wife, and son, and friend—as reaping some good even through them, a slight gain indeed, but yet a gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou make it thy chief object not to marry a rich wife, but to have a devout wife, and a religious daughter, thou shalt gain this consolation; if thou study to have thy son not rich but devout, thou shalt also gain this consolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou make these thine objects then wilt thyself be such as they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This also is part of virtue, to choose such friends, and such a wife and children.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not in vain are the oblations made for the departed, not in vain the prayers, not in vain the almsdeeds: all those things hath the Spirit ordered, wishing us to be benefited one by the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See: he is benefited, thou art benefited: because of him, thou hast despised wealth, being set on to do some generous act: both thou art the means of salvation to him, and he to thee the occasion of thine almsgiving.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Doubt not that he shall get some good thereby.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not for nothing that the Deacon cries, “For them that are fallen asleep in Christ, and for them that make the memorials for them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not the Deacon that utters this voice, but the Holy Ghost: I speak of the Gift.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is the Sacrifice in hand, and all things laid out duly ordered: Angels are there present, Archangels, the Son of God is there: all stand with such awe, and in the general silence those stand by, crying aloud: and thinkest thou that what is done, is done in vain?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then is not the rest also all in vain, both the oblations made for the Church, and those for the priests, and for the whole body?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God forbid! but all is done with faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What thinkest thou of the oblation made for the martyrs, of the calling made in that hour, martyrs though they be, yet even “for martyrs?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a great honor to be named in the presence of the Lord, when that memorial is celebrating, the dread Sacrifice, the unutterable mysteries.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as, so long as the Emperor is seated, is the time for the petitioner to effect what he wishes to effect, but when he is risen, say what he will, it is all in vain, so at that time, while the celebration of the mysteries is going on, it is for all men the greatest honor to be held worthy of mention.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For look: then is declared the dread mystery, that God gave Himself for the world: along with that mystery he seasonably puts Him in mind of them that have sinned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as when the celebration of Emperors’ victories is in progress, then, as many as had their part in the victory receive their meed of praise, while at the same time as many as are in bonds are set at liberty in honor of the occasion; but when the occasion is past, he that did not obtain this favor then, no longer gets any: so is it here likewise: this is the time of celebration of a victory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, saith it, “so often as ye eat this bread, ye do show forth the Lord’s death.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not approach indifferently, nor imagine that these things are done in any ordinary sort.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is in another sense that we make mention of martyrs, and this, for assurance that the Lord is not dead: and this, for a sign that death has received its death’s blow, that death itself is dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowing these things, let us devise what consolations we can for the departed, instead of tears, instead of laments, instead of tombs, our alms, our prayers, our oblations, that both they and we may attain unto the promised blessings, by the grace and loving-kindness of His only-begotten Son our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
